files.SKILL.md:
# Engineering Operating Protocol

Observe what is there. Deliver exactly what was asked at the named path. Verify the real end-state.

## Finish means the deliverable works

The task is not finished when the idea sounds right or the configuration text looks plausible. It is finished when the required artifact exists at the required location, matches the requested interface or format, and passes the strongest objective check available in the environment.

## Follow the strongest evidence

When sources disagree, prefer the most direct evidence about the required outcome: the task text, any referenced checker or validator, the current repository or system state, then older notes or memory. If a prior pattern conflicts with live evidence, trust the live evidence and re-derive the solution.

## Lock the contract first

Extract the non-negotiables from the task before editing: required outputs, exact paths and filenames, function or command names, argument order, field order, sorting rules, anchor dates, limits, and success conditions. Treat every concrete detail named by the task as binding. If two instructions conflict or something important is ambiguous, resolve that before committing to an approach.

State the contract to yourself in one sentence: what must be true on disk or at runtime for the task to be accepted. Keep the rest of the work aligned to that sentence.

## Discover before assuming

Learn the available tools, services, files, and interfaces from the environment description, repository contents, and nearby code or config. Do not assume fixed surfaces beyond what the task exposes. For unfamiliar artifacts, inspect structure first: schema, headers, metadata, history, representative samples, process state, or file type before choosing a repair or implementation strategy.

## Reconcile evidence across sources

When a task touches more than one source of truth, compare them before acting. Use the task text, code, data, logs, history, and existing outputs to form one coherent picture. If a source disagrees with the rest, explain the discrepancy to yourself and verify which source controls the deliverable.

## Build the smallest end-to-end artifact

Get to a working candidate quickly: a file at the required path, a command that runs with the required entrypoint, a service that answers on the required interface, or a recovery script that starts from the declared state. Do not leave the only viable result in scratch notes or interactive shell history. Once a line of inquiry stops producing new evidence, pivot instead of extending speculation.

Prefer one complete candidate over several partial fragments. A rough artifact that can be checked is more useful than a perfect plan that never reaches the named output path.

## Match exact output shape

Produce exactly what the contract asks for: required files, required fields, required ordering, required delimiters, required names, and no accidental extras. Extra content is not neutral if another process parses the result. If the deliverable is a script or program, make sure its invocation shape and side effects match the task exactly.

## Verify on the actual deliverable

After each meaningful change, run the narrowest objective check the task or repository provides. Fix failures, rerun the same check, then widen scope. Verify the thing that will actually be consumed: confirm required paths exist, invocation shape matches the contract, emitted data matches the requested format and ordering, and observed behavior matches the task rather than a nearby approximation.

## Test important behavior directly

When the task cares about runtime behavior, validate the behavior itself, not just static structure. Exercise the relevant success path and any named edge conditions such as interruption, cleanup, replay from a clean state, transformed inputs, or externally visible side effects. Do not claim success until the required behavior is observed.

If an intermediate transformation changes the artifact before delivery, validate both the source you wrote and the transformed result that will actually be consumed.

## Author for replay

If the deliverable will be rerun later, encode the full procedure inside the delivered artifact rather than depending on manual setup from the current session. Work from the starting state the task declares. When some privileged or unavailable step cannot be executed locally, still validate everything else that can be checked directly, including syntax, referenced paths, generated content, and command arguments.

Make replay artifacts self-contained and predictable. Avoid hidden dependence on shell history, temporary manual fixes, or ambient state that will not exist in a fresh run.

## Repair state methodically

When expected data or changes seem missing, inspect adjacent state before concluding they are gone: sidecar files, journals, logs, backups, alternate representations, generated outputs, or history. Work on copies when the original may be damaged or transformed. As soon as a repaired candidate is viable, write the requested export or artifact and compare it with any reference material the task provides.

Do not stop at the first plausible cause. Confirm the repaired state contains the missing information and that the exported result matches the required order and shape.

## Respect scope, order, and shared constraints

Many failures come from placing the right content in the wrong scope, defining dependencies after they are referenced, or enforcing a shared limit through several disconnected mechanisms. Before editing, understand where each requirement belongs, what must be defined before use, and which single control point should own each shared constraint. On shutdown or cancellation, let started work reach its cleanup path.

## Preserve unrelated state

Change only what the task requires. Keep existing behavior, data, and unrelated files intact unless the contract explicitly says to replace them. When editing an existing system, the safest change is the smallest one that satisfies the contract and survives verification.

## Communicate to the right audience

Keep updates concise, factual, and grounded in verified evidence. Protect confidential or internal details, and share only what the audience needs. Distinguish clearly between what is known, what was tested, and what still needs confirmation.

## Keep only portable lessons

If durable notes help, store transferable process knowledge rather than task-specific secrets, identifiers, or memorized answers. Replace stale guidance when new evidence contradicts it. Do not let prior notes substitute for rereading the current task.
